danger_signals.meaning: Player has not met qualifying time/distance threshold for current event
Player has exhausted all attempts and competition ends

danger_signals.visual_form: Audience visual in background scrolling/reaction changes
danger_signals.meaning: Spectator engagement reflects performance quality (positive for success, negative for failure)

safety_signals.meaning: Player has achieved qualifying standard and progresses safely

safety_signals.visual_form: Lives counter increment at 100,000 point intervals
safety_signals.meaning: High score performance grants additional attempts/safety buffer

safety_signals.visual_form: Large scoreboard showing world records and current attempts
safety_signals.meaning: Visual feedback of performance relative to established standards provides context for success/failure

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Running track, field equipment (hurdles, javelins, hammers)

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Different sprite designs for competing CPU athletes
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Separate lanes on horizontal split-screen display

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Lower qualifying thresholds displayed on scoreboard; accessible event parameters
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player CPU competitors perform at baseline difficulty level

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Higher qualifying thresholds appear on scoreboard after completing all six events
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Increased difficulty scaling; CPU competitors perform at higher efficiency

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Split horizontal scrolling screens showing paired competitors side-by-side
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Players 2&4 use left control set, Players 1&3 use right set; competitors race simultaneously but qualify independently

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Right control set selected; CPU fills remaining player slots
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player competes against CPU athletes; relative performances displayed but qualification based on solo qualifying times only

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Event-specific visual theming: 100m dash, long jump, javelin, hurdles, hammer throw, high jump stations
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Six-event tournament structure mimics Olympic competition format with progression mechanics

environmental_storytelling.example: Packed crowd in background serves as spectator presence; scrolling at independent rates creates depth and atmosphere of Olympic stadium setting
Hurdles appear on track for 110m hurdles event; javelin and hammer throw implements visible during respective events; high jump bar displayed during jumping event

environmental_storytelling.visual_form: Large prominent scoreboard showing world records, current player attempts, qualifying standards, and performance metrics
environmental_storytelling.example: World records contextualize player achievement; current score display motivates performance competition
Horizontal split-screen scrolling for multiple independent areas (track, audience, back wall) creates stadium environment and multiplayer space differentiation

entity_behavioral_tells.state: Running (100m dash, hurdles, long jump approach)
entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Speed determined by alternating button press frequency; faster button mashing equals faster running velocity
Timing of jump button press determines angle; holding button sets trajectory at optimal 42-degree angle
Action button timing determines throw angle; 43-degree angle represents optimal performance
Action button timing required to clear each hurdle; mistiming results in collision and performance penalty
Single run button press initiates spin; action button press timing determines release angle; 45-degree angle optimal
Computer sets approach run speed; action button held to determine angle; rapid run button presses provide additional height in air
Performs athletics tasks; player relative performance does not affect CPU qualifying status; each athlete judged against independent qualifying times only

notes: Track & Field is a pure sports simulation with minimal narrative world-state. Visual communication centers on Olympic athletic competition mechanics rather than danger/safety in traditional sense. Instead, 'danger' represents failure to qualify, while 'safety' represents successful advancement. The game's world-state is entirely determined by competition round progression and difficulty escalation. NES version combines Hyper Olympic and Hyper Sports Famicom games; regional differences exist only in title screen (European version retitled to coincide with 1992 Barcelona Olympics). Unused code indicates original arcade version featured variable scroll rates for audience and back wall at different rates, creating additional depth layering not implemented in NES version. Manifestation patterns are purely mechanical/performance-based rather than environmental or political.
